title: Vibe coding for statistical analysis using large language models.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Kohout, E., Boy, P., & Siddiq, H. (in press). We explored how nurses and students can use AI language models to help run statistical analyses, lowering the barrier to doing research.

title: Enhancing dementia caregiver self-efficacy through culturally tailored, video-based education for Hispanic caregivers: A quality improvement project.
text: Kim, A., Tolentino, D. A., Choi, S. E., & Song, Y. (2026). This study tested short, culturally tailored videos to help Hispanic family caregivers feel more confident and capable in caring for a loved one with dementia.

title: An analysis and evaluation of the theory of moral reckoning.
text: Kohout, E., Powell, M., & Tolentino, D. A. (2026). This paper examines “moral reckoning,” a nursing theory about how nurses respond when their values clash with difficult workplace situations, and weighs how useful and sound the theory is.

title: Exploring the quality of TikTok-based diabetes self-management education in English and Spanish: A digital health study.
text: Diaz, L., Tolentino, D. A., Brecht, M.-L., Robbins, W., & Choi, S. (2026). This study reviewed diabetes advice on TikTok in English and Spanish to see how accurate and trustworthy it is, since many people now turn to social media for health information.

title: Continuous glucose monitoring for glycemic control in non-insulin-treated patients with type 2 diabetes.
text: Karimi, N., Choi, S. E., Song, Y., & Tolentino, D. A. (2026). This study looked at whether wearable continuous glucose monitors help people with type 2 diabetes who do not use insulin keep their blood sugar in a healthier range.

title: Navigating type 2 diabetes care: Asian American perspectives on self-management education and support.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Boy, P., Long, S., Roca, R. P. E., III, Peña, M., Sidhu, M., Luzuriaga, M., Palao, G. M., Palao, G. J., & Choi, S. (2025). We asked Asian Americans with type 2 diabetes what helps and what gets in the way when they try to use diabetes education and support programs, so those progra

title: Colonial mentality and diabetes self-management in Filipino Americans with type 2 diabetes.
text: Roca, R. P. E., III, Albahsahli, B., Palao, G. J., Palao, G. M., Lance, D., Carpizo, K., & Tolentino, D. A. (2025). This study explored how “colonial mentality” (internalized beliefs rooted in a colonial past) relates to how Filipino Americans manage their type 2 diabetes.

title: Associations of work-family conflict with changes in metabolic risk factors: A four-year longitudinal study.
text: Saiki, M., Robbins, W., Tolentino, D. A., Macey, P., Nakata, A., & Li, J. (2024). This study followed workers over four years to see whether the stress of juggling work and family was linked to changes in health markers such as blood pressure, weight, and blood sugar.

title: Filipino Americans’ social and cultural experiences of type 2 diabetes management: Cultural paradox, ownership, and success definition.
text: Tolentino, D. A., & Byrnes, M. (2024). We talked with Filipino Americans about what living with and managing type 2 diabetes is really like day to day: how family, food, faith, and cultural expectations can either help or get in the way. The goal is care that respects and works with their culture.

title: A cross-sectional study on social determinants of mental health during COVID-19 among adults in California.
text: Tolentino, D. A., & Ajuwon, M. (2023). We looked at how everyday life circumstances, such as income, housing, and access to care, were associated with Californians’ mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic.

title: Determinants of American adults’ use of digital health and willingness to share health data with providers, family, and social media.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Costa, D., & Jiang, Y. (2023). We looked at the factors associated with U.S. adults using health apps and devices, and with their willingness to share health information with doctors, family, or on social media. This helps designers build digital health tools that people actually t

title: A qualitative analysis describing attrition from bariatric surgery to identify strategies for improving retention in patients who desire treatment.
text: Chao, G. F., Lindquist, K., Vitous, C. A., Tolentino, D. A., Delaney, L., Alimi, Y., Jafri, S., & Telem, D. A. (2023). This study explored why some patients stop short of completing the weight-loss (bariatric) surgery process. Understanding what gets in their way can help clinics support people bett

title: Economic burden, financial stress, and cost-related coping among people with uncontrolled diabetes.
text: Patel, M. R., Tolentino, D. A., Smith, A., & Heisler, M. (2023). This study examined the financial strain of living with hard-to-control diabetes, and the difficult tradeoffs people make, such as skipping medication, to cope with the cost.

title: Experiences of Filipino Americans with type 2 diabetes during COVID-19: A qualitative study.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Roca, R. P. E., III, Yang, J., Itchon, J., & Byrnes, M. (2023). We asked Filipino Americans with type 2 diabetes how the COVID-19 pandemic affected their health, including the added stress, disrupted routines and care, and what helped them cope, so that future support can be ready

title: Neonatal intensive care workflow analysis informing clinical decision support design for necrotizing enterocolitis.
text: Gephart, S. M., Tolentino, D. A., Quinn, M., Wyles, C., & Fry, E. (2023). This study mapped how nurses actually work in newborn intensive care units (NICUs). Knowing the real workflow lets hospitals design computer alerts and decision-support tools that fit how nurses work, instead of slowing them d

title: A descriptive study of nurses’ experiences with unintended consequences of the electronic health record in two urban hospitals.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Patmon, F., & Gephart, S. M. (2021). We documented the unexpected problems nurses run into with electronic health record (EHR) systems, including the workarounds, extra clicks, and frustrations, to point the way toward safer, easier-to-use systems.

title: Applying computational ethnography to examine nurses’ workflow within electronic health records.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Subbian, V., & Gephart, S. M. (2021). We used data from the electronic health record itself to study how nurses move through their digital tasks. It offers a new way to understand, and ultimately improve, the everyday computer work that takes nurses away from patients.

title: Evaluating the impact of Meducation®, a SMART application, on medication adherence: The need to evaluate EHR apps post-implementation.
text: Tolentino, D. A., & Knapp, H. (2020). We evaluated a smartphone app (Meducation) designed to help patients take their medicines correctly, and showed why health apps need to be tested after they are rolled out, not just before.

title: Type 2 diabetes self-management interventions among Asian Americans in the United States: A scoping review.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Ali, S., Jang, S. Y., Kettaneh, C., & Smith, J. (2022). We reviewed existing programs that help Asian Americans manage type 2 diabetes to see what has been tried, what works, and where the gaps are.

title: State of the science of dimensions of nurses’ user experience when using an electronic health record.
text: Tolentino, D. A., & Gephart, S. M. (2021). We summarized the research on what makes electronic health records easy or frustrating for nurses to use, to guide better, safer system design.

title: Complex identities, intersectionality, and research approaches in millennial family caregivers in the United States.
text: Aaron, S. P., Waters, A., Tolentino, D. A., Rascon, A., Phan, C., Chen, E., Travers, J., Jones, M. G., Kent-Marvick, J., & Thomas Hebdon, M. (2022). This commentary argues that research on young (millennial) family caregivers must account for their complex, overlapping identities in order to truly u

title: COVID-19 pandemic and the push to promote and include nurses in public health policy.
text: Morone, J., Tolentino, D. A., Aronowitz, S. V., & Siddiq, H. (2022). This commentary makes the case that nurses’ frontline expertise should have a stronger voice in shaping public health policy, a lesson underscored by COVID-19.

title: Nurses should oppose police violence and unjust policing in healthcare.
text: Jeffers, K. S., Siddiq, H., Martinez-Hollingsworth, A. S., Aronowitz, S. V., Sinko, L., Travers, J. L., Tolentino, D. A., Burns, J., Bell, S. A., Beck, D. C., Gutierrez, J. I., Jr., Bulgin, D., Manges, K. A., Mansfield, L. N., Bettencourt, A. P., Jun, J., Fauer, A. J., Lipsky, R. K., Adynski, G. I.,

title: Understanding the impact of culture and environment on Filipino healthcare workers during COVID-19.
text: Blancia, A., Cagulada, M. N., Cruz, B., Añel, E., Cua, A., Monreal, G. P., Lance, D., & Tolentino, D. A. (2026). Filipino healthcare workers were hit especially hard by COVID-19, facing high exposure, racist rhetoric, and inadequate protection, often hidden because data lump Filipinos into a broad “

title: Wearable use and health outcomes among older adults with chronic conditions.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Boy, P., & Matsuo, Y. (2026). Most older adults live with more than one chronic condition, and wearables are often suggested to help. Using a national survey of 910 adults aged 65 and older, this study examined whether wearable use was associated with more physical activity and gre

title: Colonialism in healthcare: Experiences of Filipino Americans.
text: Long, S. N., Boy, P., Aguinaldo, T., Dablo, H., Atienza, E., Sevillano, L., & Tolentino, D. A. (2026). Filipino Americans often face health challenges despite social advantages. Drawing on interviews with 23 Filipino American adults, this study explores how “colonial mentality,” internalized beliefs

title: Artificial intelligence in diabetes self-management: An integrative review.
text: Le, M., Bilog, A. D., Long, S. N., Tonthat, J., Siatong, P., & Tolentino, D. A. (2026). Can AI safely help people manage diabetes? Our team reviewed 25 studies on patient-facing AI tools, like chatbots and large language models. These tools show real promise, but some give inaccurate or unsafe advic

title: A machine learning approach integrating social determinants of health and allostatic load to predict type 2 diabetes.
text: Tolentino, D. A. (2026). Diabetes risk tools usually rely on clinical numbers and ignore people’s social circumstances and chronic stress. This study built a machine-learning model that added social determinants of health and “allostatic load” (the body’s cumulative stress) to predict dysglycemia. I

title: Bridging the gap: opportunities and barriers in diabetes self-management education.
text: Boy, P., Long, S., Sidhu, M., Roces, B., & Tolentino, D. A. (2025). This study asked Asian Americans with type 2 diabetes what helps and what gets in the way when using diabetes education and support. Barriers included cost, logistics, stigma, and negative provider experiences; family, community, an

title: Understanding the impact of culture and environment on Filipino healthcare workers during COVID-19.
text: Monreal, G. P., Blancia, A., Lance, D., Cagulada, M. N., Añel, E., Cua, A., Cruz, B., & Tolentino, D. A. (2025). Filipino healthcare workers were hit especially hard by COVID-19, facing high exposure, racist rhetoric, and inadequate protection, often hidden because data lump Filipinos into a broad “

title: Can sense of coherence protect the body? Salutogenic pathways to physiological stress.
text: Tolentino, D. A., & Masum, M. (2025). Chronic stress wears on the body (measured as “allostatic load”) and raises diabetes risk. This study asks whether a strong “sense of coherence,” feeling that life is understandable, manageable, and meaningful, helps buffer that physical toll. Part of the lab’s

title: Understanding the impact of culture and environment on Filipino healthcare workers during COVID-19.
text: Monreal, G. P., Blancia, A., Lance, D., Cagulada, M. N., Añel, E., Cua, A., Cruz, B., & Tolentino, D. A. (2025). Filipino healthcare workers were hit especially hard by COVID-19, facing high exposure, racist rhetoric, and inadequate protection, often hidden because data lump Filipinos into a broad “

title: From experience to biology: an analysis of multi-domain perceived stress and allostatic load.
text: Tolentino, D. A., & Masum, M. (2025). Stress from systemic disadvantages can leave a physical mark. Using the AI-READI dataset, this study examines whether perceived stress is associated with higher “allostatic load” (the body’s cumulative wear and tear) among people with type 2 diabetes. Part of th

title: Cost-related maladaptive coping and diabetes distress among adults with diabetes.
text: Wang, C., Patel, M. R., & Tolentino, D. A. (2025). Diabetes is expensive, and some people cope in harmful ways like skipping medications. This study examined whether such cost-related coping is associated with “diabetes distress” (the emotional weight of diabetes) among adults with uncontrolled diab

title: Geospatial mapping of diabetes programs and equity index among Asian Americans in Los Angeles.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Roca, R. P. E., III, Lance, D., Peña, M., Palao, G. M., Palao, G. J., Carpizo, K., Adlao, A., & Horne, R. (2024). Diabetes hits Asian Americans hard, yet fewer than one in ten join diabetes education and support programs. This study mapped where those programs sit across Los Angele

title: The relationship between task-technology fit and nurses’ performance in the EHR.
text: Tolentino, D. A., Insel, K., & Gephart, S. M. (2020). The electronic health record works best when it fits the nurse and the task. Surveying 95 nurses, this study measured that “task-technology fit” and how it relates to workload, time in the system, and performance, to help improve the everyday com

title: User experience to digital health adoption: A conceptual framework.
text: Tolentino, D. A., & Gephart, S. M. (2019). Hospitals invest heavily in tools like the electronic health record, but they only help if nurses have a good experience using them. This poster offers a framework linking nurses’ user experience to whether a system is truly adopted, and argues for designin

title: Class of 2026: celebrating Dr. Lisa Diaz and Dr. Ralph Fontejon
text: June 13, 2026. At the UCLA School of Nursing 2026 commencement on June 13, the lab celebrated its newest graduates, with special congratulations to Dr. Lisa Diaz (PhD) and Dr. Ralph Fontejon (DNP) . We are incredibly proud of all they have accomplished and excited for everything ahead of them.

He is passionate about working with the Filipino community, and his research interests include HIV prevention and treatment and the social determinants of health. In his free time

title: Gianpiero Monreal
text: Former Undergraduate Researcher. Bio coming soon.

title: Colonial mentality and health
text: UCLA Racial and Social Justice Seed Grant · Principal Investigator · 2024–2025 Grounded in Sikolohiyang Pilipino (Filipino psychology), this study uses qualitative description to understand how the social, cultural, political, and historical meanings of colonial mentality shape F

title: Discrimination and allostatic load
text: UCLA Hellman Fellowship · Principal Investigator · 2025–2026 This project examines how discrimination is associated with chronic physiological stress, measured by allostatic load, in people with type 2 diabetes, and whether socioeconomic resilience factors can buffer that associa

title: Technology-enabled self-management
text: UCLA School of Nursing Intramural Grant · Principal Investigator · 2025 Explores patient-centered digital health technologies such as wearables for older adults with multiple chronic conditions. The work describes their experiences (a qualitative descriptive study) and investigat

title: Reimagining type 2 diabetes self-management to tackle cost and access barriers
text: Physician’s Weekly Dr. Tolentino makes the case that diabetes self-management education and support, one of the most effective tools for controlling type 2 diabetes, too often stays out of reach for Asian American communities because of cost and limited access. He calls for rethinking how these prog

title: Beyond the statute: why health care needs systemic reform to promote equity
text: Daily Bruin In this Daily Bruin story on equity in health care, Dr. Tolentino explains why closing health disparities takes more than new policies. He argues for race-conscious care that names racism as a health factor and shifts decision-making power toward the communities most affected.

title: Nurse leader advocates for integrating informatics into healthcare
text: The Global Filipino Magazine This profile highlights Dr. Tolentino’s call to weave informatics and digital health skills throughout nursing education and practice. He encourages nurses to explore careers beyond the bedside and to use technology to close gaps in care.

title: Nursing informatics and health equity
text: Clinical Appraisal Podcast Dr. Tolentino joins the Clinical Appraisal Podcast to talk about his path into nursing informatics as a nurse informaticist, and how data and digital tools can be used to advance health equity.

title: Managing diabetes among Filipinos in the diaspora
text: Positively Filipino Written for the Filipino diaspora, this article offers practical, culturally grounded tips for living well with diabetes, like adapting beloved Filipino dishes with healthier swaps such as cauliflower rice. The takeaway: steady self-management, regular movement, and a strong part

title: About the TenJ Lab
text: Nursing science and informatics for equitable diabetes prevention and care for marginalized communities, including Filipino Americans. The story behind Ten J, honoring Jessie Tolentino.
